original_hypothesis.title: approveAndCall External Callback Enables Token Drain via Reentrancy
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Vultisig.approveAndCall sets ERC20 allowance via _approve BEFORE making an external call to IApproveAndCallReceiver(spender).receiveApproval(). The spender contract can re-enter and call transferFrom to drain the full approved amount within the callback, before the user's transaction completes. This follows the classic approve-then-callback anti-pattern without reentrancy protection.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function violates checks-effects-interactions by making an external call to an arbitrary contract after setting state (approval). While the user voluntarily calls this function, the spender contract could perform unexpected actions within the callback that the user didn't anticipate when constructing the transaction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _approve(msg.sender, spender, amount) at line 15; external call IApproveAndCallReceiver(spender).receiveApproval at line 18 — approve before external call

preconditions: User calls approveAndCall with a contract address as spender
Spender contract implements receiveApproval to call transferFrom within callback
User does not expect the spender to drain tokens during the callback

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _approve sets allowance, then receiveApproval is called — spender can transferFrom within callback
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious spender contract receives approval callback and immediately transfers all approved tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker deploys malicious receiver contract; tricks users into approveAndCall; drains full approved amount

economic_questions: Is this the intended design pattern for approveAndCall?
Are there any composability risks where a legitimate protocol's receiveApproval implementation is exploitable?
Does the VultisigWhitelisted _beforeTokenTransfer hook add any protection?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious receiver that drains tokens in callback
Test with whitelist active to see if transfers are blocked
Check if any legitimate integrations use approveAndCall

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas to deploy malicious receiver (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to full VULT balance of victim
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on victim balance and social engineering success

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time user can be convinced to call approveAndCall

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Individual users who call approveAndCall with malicious spender
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on number and balances of victims

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review whether any legitimate protocols integrate via approveAndCall

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
